articles.title: Scientists Find a Protein That Can Cause Blindness in Diabetes
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered a protein called LRG1 that can cause eye problems in people with diabetes. This eye disease is called diabetic retinopathy and can lead to blindness. The protein makes tiny blood vessels in the eye squeeze too tight, which stops oxygen from reaching the retina. This starts a chain reaction that damages vision over time. In tests with diabetic mice, blocking LRG1 stopped the early eye damage and kept their eyes healthy. This finding could help doctors create new treatments to prevent blindness before it starts, instead of just fixing damage after it happens. The research was done by scientists at UCL and supported by charities like Diabetes UK.

articles.title: Ancient Sheep Helps Solve a Mystery of an Old Plague
articles.summary: A long time ago, a sickness called plague made many people very sick. Scientists have found a clue about how this plague spread thousands of years ago. They discovered the plague bacteria in the bones of a sheep that lived 4,000 years ago in Russia. This sheep was from a place called Arkaim. The scientists think that animals like sheep might have helped the plague move across big areas. This is important because it helps us understand how diseases can travel. The research was done by a team from different countries, and they published their findings in a science journal. By studying old animal bones, they learn about history and health.

articles.title: Antarctica's Gravity Mystery: Why It's Weaker There
articles.summary: Gravity is the force that pulls things down, but it's not the same everywhere on Earth. Scientists found that Antarctica has a special spot where gravity is weaker, called a gravity hole. This happens because rocks deep inside the Earth move very slowly over millions of years, making the ground less dense. The weaker gravity makes the ocean around Antarctica sit a bit lower. Researchers used earthquake waves, like a CT scan for Earth, to study this. They think this gravity change might be linked to Antarctica's ice sheets forming long ago. Understanding this helps us learn how Earth's inside affects our climate and oceans.

articles.title: Scientists See Tiny Magnetic Swirls in Super-Thin Material
articles.summary: Scientists have seen tiny magnetic swirls in a very thin material for the first time. They cooled a material called nickel phosphorus trisulfide to very cold temperatures, and it formed special magnetic patterns called vortices. These vortices are like little whirlpools where the magnetic parts of atoms spin in circles. The scientists also saw the material change into another magnetic state when it got even colder. This discovery was predicted by scientists 50 years ago. It could help make very small technology in the future, like tiny computers or devices that use magnetism. The research was done by physicists at The University of Texas at Austin and published in a science journal.

articles.title: Moss Helps Catch Grave Robbers in a Cemetery
articles.summary: At Burr Oak Cemetery in Chicago, some workers were caught digging up old graves to sell the spaces again, which is called grave robbing. This happened in 2009, and they were caught in 2015. Scientists used moss, a small green plant, to help prove when the graves were disturbed. Moss can tell time because it changes as it gets older, even when buried. The FBI asked a museum expert to study the moss found with the bones. This helped show when the robbers moved the remains. The cemetery is special because famous people like Emmett Till are buried there. This story shows how tiny plants can help solve big crimes.

articles.title: How Animals See: The Story of Our Eyes
articles.summary: Scientists have a new idea about how our eyes evolved. Long ago, our animal ancestors might have lost their original eyes because they lived buried in mud and didn't need them. Later, when they started swimming again, they needed eyes to see and steer. They didn't have the old eyes anymore, so they used a single light-sensing organ in the middle of their head to make two new eyes. This is why vertebrate eyes, like ours, are different from those of insects and squids. The study suggests that our eyes were 'reinvented' from this single organ, leading to the unique structure we have today. This helps explain why our eyes work differently from other animals' eyes.
